title: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess

items.subtopic: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess
items.title: Risk Factors for Multiple Nutrient Deficiencies
items.content: Multiple nutrient deficiencies are more likely to occur in individuals who are chronically ill, alcoholic, on specific medications, or living in poverty. Additionally, patients post-bariatric surgery are at high risk due to altered gastrointestinal anatomy affecting absorption.
keyPoints: Overt vitamin or trace mineral deficiencies are rare in Western countries due to food fortification and supplements.
Multiple nutrient deficiencies may appear together in chronically ill individuals, alcoholics, those on specific medications, or living in poverty.
Post-bariatric surgery patients are at high risk for multiple nutrient deficiencies.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess

items.subtopic: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess
items.title: Timeframe for Developing Vitamin Deficiencies
items.content: The time it takes to develop a vitamin deficiency varies depending on the vitamin. For example, vitamins B12 and A have large body stores, potentially delaying deficiency onset for ≥1 year after starting a deficient diet, whereas folate and thiamine can deplete within weeks.
keyPoints: Body stores of vitamins vary; vitamins B12 and A may not lead to deficiency until ≥1 year on a deficient diet.
Folate and thiamine deficiencies can develop within weeks on a deficient diet.
Therapeutic modalities like hemodialysis or diuretics can deplete water-soluble vitamins, necessitating supplementation.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess

items.title: Thiamine Deficiency in Alcoholism and Its Consequences
items.content: Alcohol interferes with thiamine absorption, synthesis of its active form, and increases urinary excretion. This can lead to acute deficiency when carbohydrates are reintroduced without adequate thiamine supplementation.
keyPoints: Thiamine is essential for energy generation through decarboxylation reactions.
Alcoholism is a primary cause of thiamine deficiency in Western countries.
Refeeding patients with alcoholism requires thiamine replenishment to prevent lactic acidosis.
Wernicke's encephalopathy and Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome are CNS manifestations of chronic thiamine deficiency.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINS | THIAMINE (VITAMIN B $ _{1} $)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Thiamine Deficiency
items.content: Thiamine deficiency can manifest as wet or dry beriberi, with cardiovascular symptoms in the former and peripheral neuropathy in the latter. CNS involvement includes Wernicke's encephalopathy.
keyPoints: Wet beriberi presents after 3 months of thiamine deficiency with cardiac enlargement and high-output heart failure.
Dry beriberi involves symmetric motor and sensory neuropathy, particularly affecting the legs.
Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome includes memory loss and confabulatory psychosis.
Transketolase activity assay is used for diagnosis; >25% stimulation by thiamine pyrophosphate indicates deficiency.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINS | THIAMINE (VITAMIN B $ _{1} $)

items.title: Risk Factors and Prevention of Thiamine Deficiency
items.content: Thiamine deficiency is more common in populations with diets high in milled rice, chronic illnesses, or certain medications. Preventive measures include supplementation for at-risk groups.
keyPoints: Diets heavy on milled polished rice increase thiamine deficiency risk.
Antithiamine factors in foods like tea and coffee can lower body stores.
At-risk populations include those with alcoholism, chronic illnesses, and certain surgeries or therapies.
Maternal deficiency can lead to infantile beriberi.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINS | THIAMINE (VITAMIN B $ _{1} $)

items.title: Management of Acute Thiamine Deficiency
items.content: In cases of acute thiamine deficiency presenting with cardiovascular or neurologic symptoms, intravenous administration of 200 mg of thiamine three times daily is recommended until no further improvement in acute symptoms is observed. This should be followed by oral thiamine (10 mg/d) until recovery is complete.
keyPoints: Intravenous thiamine: 200 mg three times daily for acute symptoms
Oral thiamine: 10 mg/day post-acute phase
Cardiovascular and ophthalmoplegic improvement within 24 hours
Consider concomitant correction of other nutrient deficiencies (e.g., magnesium)
Widespread subclinical deficiency suggests generous supplementation in emergency care and patients with alcohol use disorder
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Thiamine Deficiency | RIBOFLAVIN (VITAMIN B $ _{2} $)

items.title: Characteristics of Riboflavin Deficiency
items.content: Riboflavin deficiency is rare and typically results from dietary insufficiency. It primarily affects mucocutaneous surfaces, leading to lesions in the mouth and skin, and can also cause corneal vascularization, anemia, and personality changes.
keyPoints: Dietary sources: milk, dairy products, enriched breads/cereals, lean meat, fish, eggs, broccoli, legumes
Riboflavin is light-sensitive; protect against photodegradation
Diagnosis via red blood cell/urinary riboflavin concentrations or erythrocyte glutathione reductase activity with added FAD
Absorption capacity: ~27 mg per oral dose/meal
No reported toxicity due to instantaneous urinary excretion
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Thiamine Deficiency | RIBOFLAVIN (VITAMIN B $ _{2} $)

items.title: Understanding Pellagra: Causes and Clinical Manifestations
items.content: Pellagra is a condition caused by niacin deficiency, characterized by dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia, and potentially death. It is prevalent among populations with corn-based diets or those experiencing malnutrition due to alcoholism, Hartnup disease, carcinoid syndrome, or isoniazid therapy.
keyPoints: Pellagra causes: niacin deficiency, often seen in corn-based diets (China, Africa, India), alcoholics, Hartnup disease, carcinoid syndrome, and isoniazid therapy.

Symptoms: loss of appetite, generalized weakness, irritability, abdominal pain, vomiting, bright red glossitis, characteristic skin rash (Casal's necklace), vaginitis, esophagitis, diarrhea, depression, seizures, dementia.
The four Ds of pellagra: dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia, death.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · NIACIN (VITAMIN B $ _{3} $)

items.title: Niacin Metabolism and Conversion from Tryptophan
items.content: Nicotinic acid and nicotinamide are precursors to NAD and NADP, essential for oxidation-reduction reactions. Tryptophan can be converted to niacin at a 60:1 efficiency ratio, with conversion influenced by vitamin B6, riboflavin, iron levels, and isoniazid.
keyPoints: Niacin forms: nicotinic acid and nicotinamide; precursors of NAD and NADP.
Tryptophan to niacin conversion efficiency: 60:1 by weight.
Factors affecting tryptophan conversion: vitamin B6, riboflavin, iron deficiencies, isoniazid presence.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · NIACIN (VITAMIN B $ _{3} $)

items.title: Treatment and Dosage Guidelines for Pellagra
items.content: Oral supplementation with nicotinamide or nicotinic acid is the primary treatment for pellagra. The recommended dosage ranges from 100–200 mg three times daily for up to 4 weeks.
keyPoints: Recommended oral supplementation: 100–200 mg of nicotinamide or nicotinic acid, three times daily
Treatment duration: Up to 4 weeks
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Pellagra

items.title: Toxicity and Side Effects of Nicotinic Acid
items.content: Nicotinic acid can cause prostaglandin-mediated flushing at doses as low as 30 mg. Other side effects include skin dryness, itching, paresthesia, headache, nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. Hepatic toxicity is a serious concern with sustained-release forms, potentially leading to jaundice and elevated liver enzymes.
keyPoints: Flushing can occur at doses as low as 30 mg
Serious hepatic toxicity may present with jaundice and elevated AST/ALT levels
Upper limit for daily (nontherapeutic) niacin intake: 35 mg
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Pellagra

items.title: Recognizing Vitamin B₆ Deficiency
items.content: Vitamin B₆ deficiency can lead to significant clinical symptoms, including peripheral neuropathy and microcytic hypochromic anemia due to its role in heme synthesis.
keyPoints: Severe vitamin B₆ deficiency leads to peripheral neuropathy, abnormal electroencephalograms, and personality changes like depression and confusion.
Microcytic hypochromic anemia results from diminished hemoglobin synthesis as aminolevulinate synthase requires PLP.
Deficiency is diagnosed with plasma PLP values <20 nmol/L.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B $ _{6} $)

items.title: Managing Medication-Induced Vitamin B₆ Deficiency
items.content: Certain medications can induce vitamin B₆ deficiency, necessitating concurrent supplementation to prevent neuropathy.
keyPoints: Medications like isoniazid interact with PLP and may cause deficiency; pyridoxine should be given concurrently to avoid neuropathy.
Vitamin B₆ deficiency related to medication use is treated with 100–200 mg/d.
Vitamin B₆ should not be administered with L-dopa due to interference.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B $ _{6} $)

items.title: Understanding Vitamin B₆ Toxicity
items.content: High doses of vitamin B₆ can lead to toxicity, primarily causing sensory neuropathy.
keyPoints: The safe upper limit for vitamin B₆ is 100 mg/d; no adverse effects from food sources only.
Toxicity causes severe sensory neuropathy, which may be reversible upon cessation.
Long-term high-dose supplementation should be discouraged due to potential neuropathy.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B $ _{6} $)

items.title: Vitamin C Absorption Dynamics
items.content: Vitamin C absorption is dose-dependent, with nearly complete absorption at doses under 100 mg. However, absorption efficiency decreases significantly for doses exceeding 1 g.
keyPoints: Absorption of vitamin C is almost complete if <100 mg is administered in a single dose.
Only ≤50% of vitamin C is absorbed at doses >1 g.
Higher intake levels lead to enhanced degradation and increased fecal and urinary excretion.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FOLATE (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $) | VITAMIN C

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Management of Vitamin C Deficiency
items.content: Vitamin C deficiency, leading to scurvy, presents with symptoms related to impaired connective tissue formation. Diagnosis is confirmed by low plasma or leukocyte levels, and treatment involves vitamin C supplementation.
keyPoints: Scurvy results from vitamin C deficiency, primarily affecting the poor, elderly, alcoholics consuming <10 mg/d, and those on unbalanced diets.
Symptoms include bleeding into skin, inflamed gums, joint bleeding, and impaired bone growth in children.
Low plasma or leukocyte levels confirm diagnosis.
Administering 200 mg/d of vitamin C improves scurvy symptoms within days.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FOLATE (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $) | VITAMIN C

items.title: Toxicity and Precautions with High-Dose Vitamin C Supplementation
items.content: High doses of vitamin C can cause gastrointestinal distress, and there are concerns about kidney stones and iron overload. Patients with certain conditions should avoid large doses.
keyPoints: Doses >2 g may result in abdominal pain, diarrhea, and nausea.

Chronic high-dose supplementation is feared to increase kidney stone prevalence due to oxalate metabolism, though this has not been consistently observed except in those with preexisting renal disease.
Patients with a history of kidney stones or renal insufficiency should avoid large doses.
High doses can cause false-negative guaiac reactions and interfere with certain diagnostic tests.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FOLATE (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $) | VITAMIN C

items.title: Recognizing Biotin Deficiency: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis
items.content: Biotin deficiency, though rare due to low dietary intake, is more commonly caused by metabolic errors or specific dietary conditions. In adults, it manifests as mental changes (e.g., depression, hallucinations), paresthesia, anorexia, nausea, and a distinctive rash around the eyes, nose, mouth, and extremities. Infants may show hypotonia, lethargy, apathy, alopecia, and a characteristic rash including the ears.
keyPoints: Biotin deficiency is rare from diet alone; more common with metabolic errors (e.g., biotinidase deficiency).
Adult symptoms: mental changes, paresthesia, anorexia, nausea, scaling, seborrheic, erythematous rash around eyes/nose/mouth and extremities.
Infant symptoms: hypotonia, lethargy, apathy, alopecia, characteristic rash including ears.
Diagnosis involves decreased urinary biotin/metabolites, increased 3-hydroxyisovaleric acid after leucine challenge, or decreased activity of biotin-dependent enzymes in lymphocytes.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · BIOTIN

items.title: Biotin Supplementation and Laboratory Interference: Treatment and Considerations
items.content: Treatment for biotin deficiency requires pharmacologic doses up to 10 mg/d, with no known toxicity. However, high-dose biotin supplements can interfere with immunoassays using streptavidin-biotin technology, leading to false-positive or negative results in tests such as free T4/T3, thyroid-stimulating hormone, troponin, and beta-hCG pregnancy test.
keyPoints: Treatment requires pharmacologic doses of biotin up to 10 mg/d with no known toxicity.
High-dose biotin can interfere with immunoassays using streptavidin-biotin technology.
Potential false-positive/negative results in tests: free T4/T3, thyroid-stimulating hormone, troponin, beta-hCG pregnancy test.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · BIOTIN

items.title: Understanding Pantothenic Acid Deficiency and Its Clinical Implications
items.content: Pantothenic acid, a component of coenzyme A, is crucial for fatty acid metabolism and synthesis of cholesterol and steroid hormones. Human deficiency is rare but can cause nonspecific symptoms like gastrointestinal disturbance, depression, muscle cramps, paresthesia, ataxia, and hypoglycemia.
keyPoints: Pantothenic acid is involved in the acetylation of proteins and excreted in urine.
Deficiency diagnosed by low urinary vitamin levels.
Symptoms include gastrointestinal disturbance, depression, muscle cramps, paresthesia, ataxia, hypoglycemia.
Historical example: 'burning feet syndrome' in WWII prisoners.
No reported toxicity.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PANTOTHENIC ACID (VITAMIN B.) | CHOLINE

items.title: Choline's Role and Clinical Considerations
items.content: Choline is essential for cell membrane integrity, neurotransmission, and lipid metabolism. Deficiency can lead to fatty liver and muscle damage, while toxicity may cause hypotension and a fishy odor.
keyPoints: Recommended intake: 550 mg/d for men, 425 mg/d for women.
Deficiency linked to fatty liver, elevated aminotransferase levels, skeletal muscle damage with high creatine phosphokinase values.
Diagnosis based on low plasma levels; nonspecific conditions may also suppress levels.
Toxicity symptoms: hypotension, increased sweating, diarrhea, salivation, fishy body odor.
Upper intake limit set at 3.5 g/d.
Potential therapeutic use in dementia and cardiovascular risk reduction, though benefits not firmly documented.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PANTOTHENIC ACID (VITAMIN B.) | CHOLINE

items.title: Dietary Sources and Potential Health Benefits of Flavonoids
items.content: Flavonoids are polyphenolic compounds found in various fruits, vegetables, and legumes. They have been studied for their potential role in preventing chronic diseases due to their antioxidant properties and effects on cell signaling.
keyPoints: Major groups include anthocyanidins (berries), flavanols (green tea, chocolate), flavonols (broccoli, kale, onions, grapes, apples), isoflavones (legumes).
Dietary intake of flavonoids ranges from 10–400 mg/d; this may be an underestimate.
Flavonoids have been linked to potential prevention of neurodegenerative disease, diabetes, and osteoporosis in observational studies.
The consistent importance of flavonoids against human diseases is not yet demonstrated. Encouraging a diet rich in fruits, vegetables, and legumes can increase intake of these bioactives.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FLAVONOIDS

items.title: Bioavailability and Metabolism of Isoflavones
items.content: Isoflavones, a type of flavonoid found primarily in legumes, have low bioavailability and are partially metabolized by intestinal flora. Despite this, they are included in discussions on the potential health benefits of dietary flavonoids.
keyPoints: Isoflavones have low bioavailability and undergo partial metabolism by intestinal flora.
Despite limited bioavailability, isoflavones are part of studies exploring the role of flavonoids in disease prevention.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FLAVONOIDS

items.title: Understanding Vitamin A Forms and Their Functions
items.content: Vitamin A encompasses retinol, retinaldehyde, and retinoic acid. Retinaldehyde is essential for vision, while retinoic acid is crucial for morphogenesis, growth, and cell differentiation.
keyPoints: Retinol and its esters are pre-formed vitamin A forms.
Retinaldehyde (11-cis) is necessary for normal vision.
Retinoic acid is required for morphogenesis, growth, and cell differentiation.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 1)

items.title: Metabolism and Storage of Vitamin A
items.content: The liver stores ~90% of vitamin A reserves as retinol. It is secreted in the form of a complex with retinol-binding protein, preventing renal filtration.
keyPoints: ~90% of vitamin A reserves are stored in the liver.
Vitamin A circulates bound to retinol-binding protein and transthyretin.
This complex prevents kidney glomerulus filtration.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 1)

items.title: Dietary Sources and Vitamin A Equivalency
items.content: Vitamin A is found as retinyl esters and provitamin A carotenoids. The RAE system quantifies vitamin A value: 1 µg of retinol, 12 µg of β-carotene, or 24 µg of other carotenoids.
keyPoints: Retinyl esters and provitamin A carotenoids are dietary forms of vitamin A.
RAE system: 1 RAE = 1 µg retinol, 12 µg β-carotene, or 24 µg other carotenoids.
Older literature used IUs: 1 µg retinol = 3.33 IU, 20 IU for β-carotene.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 1)

items.title: Assessment and Impact of Vitamin A Deficiency
items.content: Vitamin A deficiency is prevalent in regions with poor diets, particularly affecting preschool-age children. It can lead to xerophthalmia, increasing the risk of blindness and death from infections.
keyPoints: Serum retinol levels below 0.70 μmol/L (20 μg/dL) indicate deficiency.
190 million preschool-age children worldwide have vitamin A deficiency.
>5 million children show ocular manifestations like xerophthalmia, which can lead to blindness in an estimated quarter of a million children annually.
Vitamin A deficiency increases mortality risk from infections such as diarrhea, dysentery, measles, malaria, and respiratory diseases by 23–34% with supplementation.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 2)

items.title: Factors Influencing Vitamin A Status
items.content: Several factors can affect vitamin A status, including dietary intake, concurrent nutrient deficiencies, and certain medications.
keyPoints: Exclusive breastfeeding may not provide sufficient vitamin A if the mother is deficient or has diseases.
Zinc deficiency interferes with mobilization of vitamin A from liver stores.
Alcohol competes for alcohol (retinol) dehydrogenase, affecting retinaldehyde conversion in the eye.
Drugs like mineral oil, neomycin, and bile acid sequestrants can interfere with vitamin A absorption.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 2)

items.title: Regional Differences in Vitamin A Supplementation Efficacy
items.content: The effectiveness of vitamin A supplementation varies by region, reflecting differences in baseline deficiency risks.
keyPoints: In South Asia, a single oral dose of 50,000 IU of vitamin A shortly after birth reduces infant mortality by ≥10%.
No significant effect on infant mortality was observed with similar supplementation in African settings less affected by maternal vitamin A deficiency.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 2)

items.title: Treatment and Prevention of Vitamin A Deficiency
items.content: Vitamin A deficiency should be treated with specific dosages depending on the condition. For any stage of xerophthalmia, administer 60 mg or 200,000 IU in oily solution, repeated at 1 and 14 days later. Doses are halved for patients aged 6–11 months. Mothers with night blindness or Bitot's spots should receive 3 mg daily orally for at least 3 months.
keyPoints: 60 mg (or RAE) or 200,000 IU of vitamin A in oily solution is used to treat xerophthalmia.
Doses are repeated on day 1 and 14; reduced by half for patients aged 6–11 months.
Mothers with night blindness or Bitot's spots should receive 3 mg daily orally for at least 3 months.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 1)

items.title: Prevention Strategies in High-Risk Areas
items.content: To prevent vitamin A deficiency, supplementation is recommended every 4–6 months for young children aged 6 months to 5 years. Infants 6–11 months should receive 30 mg; children 12–59 months should receive 60 mg of vitamin A.
keyPoints: Supplementation every 4–6 months for children 6 months to 5 years in high-risk areas.
Infants 6–11 months receive 30 mg, and children 12–59 months receive 60 mg.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 1)

items.title: Management of Vitamin A Deficiency in Special Populations
items.content: Extremely low-birth-weight infants (<1000-g) should receive vitamin A supplementation of 1500 µg three times a week for 4 weeks. Children with severe measles should be given two doses of 60 mg on consecutive days.
keyPoints: Extremely low-birth-weight infants (<1000-g) require 1500 µg three times a week for 4 weeks.
Children hospitalized with severe measles receive two 60-mg doses on consecutive days.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 1)

items.title: Acute Vitamin A Toxicity: Recognizing Symptoms and Dosing Limits
items.content: Acute vitamin A toxicity can occur with high doses, leading to serious symptoms such as increased intracranial pressure, vertigo, diplopia, seizures, and potentially death. It is important to recognize the dosing thresholds that lead to acute toxicity.
keyPoints: Acute toxicity occurs after 150 mg in adults or 100 mg in children.
Symptoms include increased intracranial pressure, vertigo, diplopia, bulging fontanels (in children), seizures, and exfoliative dermatitis.
Transient symptoms in treated children: bulging of fontanels in 2% of infants; nausea, vomiting, headache in 5% of preschoolers.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 2)

items.title: Chronic Vitamin A Toxicity and Pregnancy Considerations
items.content: Chronic vitamin A toxicity is a concern with prolonged intake above recommended levels, leading to various health issues. Special precautions are necessary during pregnancy due to the risk of congenital malformations.
keyPoints: Chronic intoxication occurs at 15 mg/d in adults and 6 mg/d in children over months.
Manifestations include dry skin, cheilosis, glossitis, vomiting, alopecia, bone demineralization, hypercalcemia, lymph node enlargement, pseudotumor cerebri, liver fibrosis with portal hypertension.
Pregnancy: daily dose should not exceed 3 mg; avoid topical retinoids and contraception for at least 1 year post-13-cis-retinoic acid use.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 2)

items.title: Assessing Vitamin D Status: Key Thresholds for Bone Health
items.content: Vitamin D status is typically assessed by measuring serum levels of 25-dihydroxyvitamin D (25[OH] vitamin D). While there's no consensus on optimal levels, a threshold of >20 ng/mL (≥50 nmol/L) is generally considered sufficient for good bone health. This concentration covers the requirements of 97.5% of the population.
keyPoints: Serum level of 25(OH) vitamin D >20 ng/mL (≥50 nmol/L) is adequate for bone health.
This threshold meets the needs of 97.5% of the population.
No consensus on optimal serum levels or benefits of screening in asymptomatic adults.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIND

items.title: Risk Factors and Prevention Strategies for Vitamin D Deficiency
items.content: Old age, lack of sun exposure, dark skin, fat malabsorption, obesity, and gastric bypass surgery are risk factors for vitamin D deficiency. In African populations, especially women, newborns, urban residents, and those in northern regions, deficiency prevalence is high.
keyPoints: Risk factors include old age, lack of sun exposure, dark skin, fat malabsorption, obesity, and gastric bypass surgery.
High prevalence in certain African demographics: women, newborns, urban populations, and northern African residents.
Prevention includes adequate intake through diet/supplements and suberythemal sun exposure.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIND

items.title: Managing Vitamin D Deficiency and Toxicity
items.content: For deficiency, treatment involves oral administration of 50,000 IU/week for 6–8 weeks followed by a maintenance dose of 800 IU/d. The upper limit of intake is set at 4000 IU/d to prevent toxicity, which can cause high plasma levels of 1,25(OH)2 vitamin D and calcium.
keyPoints: Treatment: 50,000 IU/week for 6–8 weeks, then maintenance dose of 800 IU/d.
Upper intake limit is 4000 IU/d to prevent toxicity.
Toxicity features high plasma levels of 1,25(OH)2 vitamin D and calcium.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIND

items.title: Role and Mechanism of Vitamin E in Antioxidant Defense
items.content: Vitamin E, specifically α-tocopherols, serves as a chain-breaking antioxidant protecting low-density lipoproteins and polyunsaturated fats from oxidation. It works synergistically with other antioxidants like vitamin C and glutathione to maintain its reduced state.
keyPoints: Acts as a peroxyl radical scavenger
Protects against lipid peroxidation in membranes
Synergizes with vitamin C, glutathione, and enzymes for antioxidant defense
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINE

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Diagnosis of Vitamin E Deficiency
items.content: Vitamin E deficiency is rare in developed countries but can occur due to malabsorption issues. It leads to axonal degeneration, presenting as areflexia, ataxic gait, decreased vibration and position sensations, and may include ophthalmoplegia, myopathy, and retinopathy.
keyPoints: Seen in severe malabsorptive diseases like celiac disease or after bariatric surgery
Characterized by axonal degeneration of large myelinated axons
Clinical features: areflexia, ataxic gait, decreased vibration and position sensations
Laboratory diagnosis: low blood levels of α-tocopherol (<5 μg/mL or <0.8 mg/g total lipids)
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINE

items.title: Treatment and Dosing for Vitamin E Deficiency
items.content: Symptomatic vitamin E deficiency requires treatment with specific dosages of $\alpha$-tocopherol. Patients with abetalipoproteinemia may need significantly higher doses, while children can be treated orally or intramuscularly.
keyPoints: 800–1200 mg/day of $\alpha$-tocopherol for symptomatic deficiency
5000–7000 mg/d for patients with abetalipoproteinemia
Children: 400 mg/d orally or 2 mg/kg per day intramuscularly
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin E Deficiency | VITAMINK

items.title: Risks and Contraindications of High-Dose Vitamin E
items.content: High doses of vitamin E (>800 mg/d) can reduce platelet aggregation, interfere with vitamin K metabolism, and increase mortality rates and prostate cancer risk, especially when combined with selenium.
keyPoints: Doses >800 mg/d may reduce platelet aggregation
Interference with vitamin K metabolism
Contraindicated in patients on warfarin or antiplatelet agents
>400 mg/d increases all-cause mortality and prostate cancer risk
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin E Deficiency | VITAMINK

items.title: Potential Benefits of Vitamin E
items.content: While vitamin E may have protective effects against certain conditions in premature infants, its efficacy in improving sexual performance, treating intermittent claudication, and preventing cardiovascular disease or cancer is not well-supported.
keyPoints: Protective against oxygen-induced retrolental fibroplasia, bronchopulmonary dysplasia, and intraventricular hemorrhage of prematurity
Suggested benefits for sexual performance, intermittent claudication, and aging lack evidence
May help prevent macular degeneration when combined with other antioxidants
Improves immune function in nursing home residents at doses 60–800 mg/d
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin E Deficiency | VITAMINK

items.title: Recognizing and Treating Zinc Deficiency
items.content: Zinc deficiency can lead to significant clinical manifestations, including stunted growth in children, decreased taste sensation (hypogeusia), impaired immune function, hypogonadism, dwarfism, and acrodermatitis enteropathica. Diagnosis is based on a serum zinc level <12 μmol/L (<70 μg/dL). Treatment involves oral zinc supplementation.
keyPoints: Zinc deficiency can cause stunted growth, decreased taste sensation, impaired immune function, hypogonadism, dwarfism, and acrodermatitis enteropathica.
Diagnosis is based on a serum zinc level <12 μmol/L (<70 μg/dL).
Treatment involves 60 mg of elemental zinc taken by mouth twice a day or zinc gluconate lozenges (13 mg every 2 hours while awake) for common cold symptoms.
Zinc deficiency is prevalent in developing countries and often coexists with other micronutrient deficiencies.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · ZINC | COPPER

items.title: Understanding Zinc Toxicity and Its Consequences
items.content: Acute zinc toxicity can cause nausea, vomiting, and fever. Chronic high doses of zinc (150 to 450 mg/d) may lead to immune suppression and hypochromic anemia due to secondary copper deficiency. Intranasal zinc preparations are particularly harmful, potentially causing irreversible nasal mucosa damage and anosmia.
keyPoints: Acute zinc toxicity causes nausea, vomiting, and fever.
Chronic high doses (150-450 mg/d) can depress immune function and cause hypochromic anemia due to secondary copper deficiency.
Intranasal zinc preparations may lead to irreversible nasal mucosa damage and anosmia.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · ZINC | COPPER

items.subtopic: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess
items.question: Which population is at high risk for multiple nutrient deficiencies after undergoing a specific medical procedure?
options: Patients who have undergone bariatric surgery
items.explanation: After bariatric surgery, patients are at high risk for multiple nutrient deficiencies.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess

items.subtopic: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess
items.question: Which vitamins may become depleted within weeks among those eating a deficient diet?
items.explanation: Folate and thiamine may become depleted within weeks among those eating a deficient diet.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess

items.subtopic: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess
items.question: Which therapeutic modality can deplete water-soluble vitamins from the body?
items.explanation: Hemodialysis or diuretics remove water-soluble vitamins, which must be replaced by supplementation.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a primary food source for thiamine?
items.explanation: Milled rice contains little thiamine, unlike yeast, organ meat, and pork.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINS | THIAMINE (VITAMIN B $ _{1} $)

items.question: What is a common cause of thiamine deficiency in Western countries?
items.explanation: In Western countries, alcoholism is a primary cause of thiamine deficiency.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINS | THIAMINE (VITAMIN B $ _{1} $)

items.question: Which condition can precipitate acute thiamine deficiency with lactic acidosis when carbohydrates are replenished without adequate thiamine?
items.explanation: Carbohydrate repletion without adequate thiamine in alcoholism can precipitate acute deficiency with lactic acidosis.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINS | THIAMINE (VITAMIN B $ _{1} $)

items.question: Which of the following is a CNS manifestation of chronic thiamine deficiency?
items.explanation: Horizontal nystagmus is part of Wernicke's encephalopathy, a CNS manifestation of chronic thiamine deficiency.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINS | THIAMINE (VITAMIN B $ _{1} $)

items.question: What is the recommended initial treatment for acute thiamine deficiency with cardiovascular or neurologic signs?
options: 200 mg of thiamine intravenously three times daily until no further improvement
10 mg of oral thiamine daily until recovery is complete
Immediate administration of magnesium to activate thiamine
High doses of riboflavin to support energy metabolism
items.explanation: In acute thiamine deficiency with cardiovascular or neurologic signs, initial treatment involves intravenous administration of 200 mg of thiamine three times daily.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Thiamine Deficiency | RIBOFLAVIN (VITAMIN B $ _{2} $)

items.question: Which nutrient is required for the activation of thiamine?
items.explanation: Magnesium is required for the activation of thiamine, and its deficiency should be corrected concomitantly.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Thiamine Deficiency | RIBOFLAVIN (VITAMIN B $ _{2} $)

items.question: What is a common manifestation of riboflavin deficiency?
items.explanation: Riboflavin deficiency is manifested principally by lesions of the mucocutaneous surfaces and corneal vascularization.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Thiamine Deficiency | RIBOFLAVIN (VITAMIN B $ _{2} $)

items.question: Which of the following coenzymes are derived from niacin?
items.explanation: Niacin serves as a precursor for nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD) and NAD phosphate (NADP).
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · NIACIN (VITAMIN B $ _{3} $)

items.question: What is the conversion ratio of tryptophan to niacin?
items.explanation: Tryptophan can be converted to niacin with an efficiency of 60:1 by weight.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · NIACIN (VITAMIN B $ _{3} $)

items.question: Which condition is associated with pellagra due to increased conversion of tryptophan to serotonin?
items.explanation: Pellagra is associated with carcinoid syndrome, where there is increased conversion of tryptophan to serotonin.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · NIACIN (VITAMIN B $ _{3} $)

items.question: What is the recommended oral supplementation dosage of nicotinamide or nicotinic acid for treating pellagra?
items.explanation: The recommended dosage is 100–200 mg of nicotinamide or nicotinic acid three times daily.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Pellagra

items.question: Which symptom is NOT typically associated with prostaglandin-mediated flushing due to high doses of nicotinic acid?
items.explanation: Flushing symptoms include skin dryness, itching, and headache; fever is not mentioned.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Pellagra

items.question: What is the upper limit for daily (nontherapeutic) niacin intake?
items.explanation: The upper limit for daily nontherapeutic niacin intake has been set at 35 mg.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Pellagra

items.question: Which compound is the active form of vitamin B₆ that acts as a cofactor for over 100 enzymes?
items.explanation: 5′-Pyridoxal phosphate (PLP) is the active form of vitamin B₆ that acts as a cofactor for over 100 enzymes.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B $ _{6} $)

items.question: Which condition can be treated with pyridoxine combined with doxylamine?
options: Severe nausea and vomiting in pregnancy
items.explanation: Pyridoxine combined with doxylamine can be used to treat severe nausea and vomiting in pregnancy.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B $ _{6} $)

items.question: What is the safe upper limit for daily intake of vitamin B₆?
items.explanation: The safe upper limit for vitamin B₆ has been set at 100 mg/d.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B $ _{6} $)

items.question: Which medication should not be given with L-dopa due to interference?
items.explanation: Vitamin B₆ should not be given with L-dopa, as it interferes with the action of this drug.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B $ _{6} $)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a role of vitamin C?
items.explanation: Vitamin C is involved in antioxidant activity, promotion of nonheme iron absorption, and conversion of dopamine to norepinephrine, but not in the synthesis of cholesterol.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FOLATE (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $) | VITAMIN C

items.question: What is a consequence of vitamin C deficiency?
items.explanation: Vitamin C deficiency leads to scurvy, which involves symptoms like bleeding gums and impaired connective tissue formation.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FOLATE (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $) | VITAMIN C

items.question: Which statement is true regarding the absorption of vitamin C?
options: Absorption decreases significantly when doses exceed 100 mg.
More than 90% is absorbed at doses greater than 1 g.
Absorption remains constant regardless of dose.
Less than 50% is absorbed if less than 100 mg is administered.
items.explanation: Vitamin C absorption decreases significantly when doses exceed 100 mg, with only ≤50% absorbed at doses >1 g.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FOLATE (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $) | VITAMIN C

items.question: Which condition is vitamin C supplementation NOT proven to benefit?
items.explanation: While vitamin C improves scurvy, slightly decreases symptoms of upper respiratory infections, and is useful in Chédiak-Higashi syndrome, it has not been proven to benefit hypertension.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FOLATE (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $) | VITAMIN C

items.question: Which of the following is a symptom of biotin deficiency in adults?
options: Mental changes such as depression and hallucinations
items.explanation: Biotin deficiency in adults can result in mental changes including depression and hallucinations.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · BIOTIN

items.question: What is the role of avidin found in egg white regarding biotin?
options: It enhances the absorption of biotin
It has no effect on biotin availability
It strongly binds to biotin, reducing its bioavailability
It converts biotin into an inactive form
items.explanation: Avidin in egg white strongly binds to biotin and reduces its bioavailability.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · BIOTIN

items.question: Which of the following is a diagnostic method for biotin deficiency?
options: Increased urinary excretion of 3-hydroxyisovaleric acid after a leucine challenge
items.explanation: Biotin deficiency can be diagnosed by increased urinary excretion of 3-hydroxyisovaleric acid after a leucine challenge.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · BIOTIN

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a symptom of pantothenic acid deficiency?
items.explanation: Pantothenic acid deficiency symptoms include gastrointestinal disturbance, depression, muscle cramps, paresthesia, ataxia, and hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PANTOTHENIC ACID (VITAMIN B.) | CHOLINE

items.question: Which organ is primarily responsible for the de novo synthesis of choline?
items.explanation: The liver is responsible for the de novo synthesis of choline.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PANTOTHENIC ACID (VITAMIN B.) | CHOLINE

items.question: What condition has been associated with pantothenic acid deficiency during World War II?
items.explanation: Pantothenic acid deficiency was believed to have caused the 'burning feet syndrome' seen in prisoners of war during World War II.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PANTOTHENIC ACID (VITAMIN B.) | CHOLINE

items.question: Which group of dietary flavonoids is primarily found in green tea and chocolate?
items.explanation: Flavanols, specifically catechins, are found in green tea and chocolate.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FLAVONOIDS

items.question: Which flavonoid group is known to have a low bioavailability and is partially metabolized by the intestinal flora?
items.explanation: Isoflavones have a low bioavailability and are partially metabolized by the intestinal flora.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FLAVONOIDS

items.question: What is the estimated daily dietary intake range of flavonoids?
items.explanation: The estimated daily dietary intake of flavonoids is 10–400 mg/d.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FLAVONOIDS

items.question: Which form of vitamin A is required for normal vision?
items.explanation: 11-cis-retinaldehyde (retinal) is the form of vitamin A required for normal vision.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 1)

items.question: What is the role of retinoic acid in the body?
options: Necessary for morphogenesis, growth, and cell differentiation
Acts as a precursor to β-carotene
items.explanation: Retinoic acid is necessary for normal morphogenesis, growth, and cell differentiation.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 1)

items.question: Which protein complex prevents vitamin A from being filtered by the kidney glomerulus?
items.explanation: The retinol-binding protein complex with transthyretin prevents vitamin A from being filtered by the kidney glomerulus.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 1)

items.question: How many international units (IUs) are equivalent to 1 µg of retinol?
items.explanation: 1 µg of retinol is equal to 3.33 international units (IUs).
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 1)

items.question: Which factor can interfere with vitamin A mobilization from liver stores?
items.explanation: Concurrent zinc deficiency interferes with the mobilization of vitamin A from liver stores.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 2)

items.question: What is a common ocular manifestation of vitamin A deficiency in children?
items.explanation: Xerophthalmia is an ocular manifestation of vitamin A deficiency, which includes night blindness and conjunctival xerosis.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 2)

items.question: Which method can be used to estimate total-body stores of vitamin A?
items.explanation: Stable isotopic or invasive liver biopsy methods are used to estimate total-body stores of vitamin A.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 2)

items.question: What is the normal range for serum retinol?
items.explanation: The normal range for serum retinol is 1.05–3.50 μmol/L.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended initial treatment dose for any stage of xerophthalmia?
items.explanation: The recommended dose for any stage of xerophthalmia is 60 mg (or RAE) or 200,000 IU of vitamin A.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 1)

items.question: How often should young children aged 6 months to 5 years in high-risk areas receive vitamin A supplementation?
items.explanation: Vitamin A supplementation is provided every 4–6 months to young children in high-risk areas.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended vitamin A dose for infants aged 6-11 months for prevention?
items.explanation: Infants aged 6–11 months should receive 30 mg of vitamin A for prevention.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 1)

items.question: What is the effect of high doses of $  eta $-carotene in smokers according to intervention studies?
options: It has no effect on lung cancer incidence
It prevents other types of cancers
items.explanation: Intervention studies found that high doses of $  eta $-carotene in smokers resulted in more lung cancers than placebo.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a manifestation of chronic vitamin A intoxication?
items.explanation: Chronic vitamin A intoxication manifests as dry skin, cheilosis, bone demineralization and pain, but not hyperglycemia.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended maximum daily dose of vitamin A for pregnant women?
items.explanation: The daily dose of vitamin A should not exceed 3 mg in pregnancy.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 2)

items.question: What is a potential consequence of providing excessive vitamin A to pregnant women?
items.explanation: Excessive vitamin A in pregnancy can result in spontaneous abortion and congenital malformations.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 2)

items.question: Which receptor is involved in mediating the biological effects of vitamin D?
items.explanation: The biologic effects of vitamin D are mediated by vitamin D receptors.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIND

items.question: What is the recommended daily allowance (RDA) of vitamin D for adults aged over 70 years in North America?
items.explanation: For people aged >70 years, the RDA is set at 20 µg/d (800 IU/d).
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIND

items.question: Which condition represents the classic disease of vitamin D deficiency?
items.explanation: Rickets represent the classic disease of vitamin D deficiency.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIND

items.question: What is considered a sufficient level of 25(OH) vitamin D for good bone health?
items.explanation: A 25(OH) vitamin D level of >20 ng/mL is sufficient for good bone health.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIND

items.question: Which form of vitamin E has the most biological activity in humans?
options: All stereoisomers of tocopherols and tocotrienols equally
Vitamin E does not have a specific active form
items.explanation: The RRR isomeric form of $ \alpha $-tocopherol has the most biological activity due to its affinity for the hepatic transport protein.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINE

items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation in children with vitamin E deficiency?
items.explanation: Children with vitamin E deficiency, such as those with cystic fibrosis or prolonged cholestasis, may develop areflexia and hemolytic anemia.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINE

items.question: Which condition is associated with a familial form of isolated vitamin E deficiency?
options: Defect in the α-tocopherol transport protein
High levels of vitamin C consumption
items.explanation: A familial form of isolated vitamin E deficiency is due to a defect in the α-tocopherol transport protein.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINE

items.question: What is the recommended daily dose of vitamin E for patients with abetalipoproteinemia?
items.explanation: Patients with abetalipoproteinemia may need as much as 5000–7000 mg/d of vitamin E.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin E Deficiency | VITAMINK

items.question: Which condition is NOT a known effect of high-dose vitamin E supplementation?
items.explanation: Intervention studies using vitamin E to prevent cardiovascular disease have not shown efficacy.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin E Deficiency | VITAMINK

items.question: Which of the following is a potential risk associated with high doses (>400 mg/d) of vitamin E?
items.explanation: At doses >400 mg/d, vitamin E may increase all-cause mortality rates and prostate cancer risk.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin E Deficiency | VITAMINK

items.question: In which patients is high-dose vitamin E contraindicated?
options: Patients on warfarin or antiplatelet agents
items.explanation: High doses of vitamin E may interfere with vitamin K metabolism and are contraindicated in patients taking warfarin and antiplatelet agents.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin E Deficiency | VITAMINK

items.question: Which dietary components are known to inhibit the absorption of zinc?
options: Phytate, fiber, oxalate, iron, and copper
items.explanation: Dietary phytate, fiber, oxalate, iron, and copper inhibit zinc absorption.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · ZINC | COPPER

items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation of acrodermatitis enteropathica?
options: Diarrhea, alopecia, muscle wasting, depression, irritability, and rash
items.explanation: Acrodermatitis enteropathica is characterized by diarrhea, alopecia, muscle wasting, depression, irritability, and a specific rash.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · ZINC | COPPER

items.question: What is the recommended treatment for zinc deficiency in children with diarrheal disease?
options: 60 mg of elemental zinc taken by mouth twice a day
Zinc gluconate lozenges every 2 hours while awake
items.explanation: 20 mg/d of zinc is recommended as an adjunctive therapy for diarrheal disease in children.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · ZINC | COPPER

items.subtopic: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess
items.statement: Overt vitamin or trace mineral deficiencies are common in Western countries due to a plentiful food supply.
items.explanation: Overt vitamin or trace mineral deficiencies are rare in Western countries because of a plentiful, varied, and inexpensive food supply.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess

items.subtopic: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess
items.statement: Subclinical vitamin and trace mineral deficiencies are common among the geriatric age group.
items.explanation: Subclinical vitamin and trace mineral deficiencies, often designated as 'hidden hunger,' are quite common in the normal population, especially in the geriatric age group.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess

items.statement: Thiamine deficiency is more common in cultures that rely heavily on a milled polished rice-based diet.
items.explanation: Milled rice contains little thiamine, leading to higher risk of deficiency.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINS | THIAMINE (VITAMIN B $ _{1} $)

items.statement: Thiamine is primarily involved in the synthesis of fatty acids.
items.explanation: Thiamine functions in decarboxylation of α-ketoacids and energy generation, not fatty acid synthesis.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINS | THIAMINE (VITAMIN B $ _{1} $)

items.statement: Riboflavin toxicity has been described due to its limited absorption and rapid excretion.
items.explanation: Riboflavin toxicity has not been described because of the gastrointestinal tract's limited capacity to absorb it and its instantaneous urinary excretion.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Thiamine Deficiency | RIBOFLAVIN (VITAMIN B $ _{2} $)

items.statement: Riboflavin is sensitive to light, which can lead to photodegradation.
items.explanation: Riboflavin is extremely sensitive to light, and milk should be stored in containers that protect against photodegradation.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Thiamine Deficiency | RIBOFLAVIN (VITAMIN B $ _{2} $)

items.statement: The primary manifestations of pellagra are referred to as 'the four Ds': dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia, and death.
items.explanation: 'The four Ds' refer to the primary symptoms of pellagra: dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia, leading to death.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · NIACIN (VITAMIN B $ _{3} $)

items.statement: Isoniazid can precipitate pellagra because it is a structural analogue of niacin.
items.explanation: Isoniazid, an antituberculosis drug, is a structural analogue of niacin and can lead to pellagra.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · NIACIN (VITAMIN B $ _{3} $)

items.statement: Niacin derived from food sources is associated with toxicity.
items.explanation: There is no evidence of toxicity from niacin that is derived from food sources.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Pellagra

items.statement: Hepatic toxicity can occur at high doses of sustained-release niacin.
items.explanation: Hepatic toxicity, including jaundice and elevated liver enzymes, is a serious reaction from sustained-release niacin.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Pellagra

items.statement: High doses of vitamin B₆ have been proven effective in treating premenstrual syndrome.
items.explanation: High doses of vitamin B₆ have not been found to be effective in treating premenstrual syndrome.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B $ _{6} $)

items.statement: Vitamin B₆ deficiency can lead to microcytic hypochromic anemia.
items.explanation: Microcytic hypochromic anemia in vitamin B₆ deficiency is due to diminished hemoglobin synthesis, as PLP is required by the first enzyme involved in heme biosynthesis.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B $ _{6} $)

items.statement: High doses of vitamin C can cause false-negative guaiac reactions.
items.explanation: Doses >1 g/d of vitamin C can interfere with tests for urinary glucose and cause false-negative guaiac reactions.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FOLATE (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $) | VITAMIN C

items.statement: Parenteral high-dose vitamin C is beneficial in severe infections or sepsis.
items.explanation: Parenteral high-dose vitamin C has no beneficial effects and may increase mortality in patients with severe infections or sepsis.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FOLATE (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $) | VITAMIN C

items.statement: High-dose biotin supplements are known to cause toxicity.
items.explanation: No toxicity is known from high-dose biotin supplementation.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · BIOTIN

items.statement: Biotin deficiency due to low dietary intake is common.
items.explanation: Biotin deficiency due to low dietary intake is rare; it usually results from inborn errors of metabolism.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · BIOTIN

items.statement: Toxicity from pantothenic acid has been reported.
items.explanation: No toxicity of pantothenic acid has been reported.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PANTOTHENIC ACID (VITAMIN B.) | CHOLINE

items.statement: Choline deficiency can result in fatty liver.
items.explanation: Choline deficiency results in fatty liver, elevated aminotransferase levels, and skeletal muscle damage.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PANTOTHENIC ACID (VITAMIN B.) | CHOLINE

items.statement: Flavonoids have been consistently demonstrated to prevent chronic diseases.
items.explanation: The ultimate importance and usefulness of flavonoids against human disease have not been consistently demonstrated.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FLAVONOIDS

items.statement: A dietary pattern with high intake of fruits, vegetables, and legumes is encouraged for higher intake of nonnutritive bioactives.
items.explanation: Such a dietary pattern ensures a higher intake of flavonoids and other nonnutritive bioactives.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FLAVONOIDS

items.statement: Retinoic acid functions directly in vision.
items.explanation: Retinoic acid does not function directly in vision.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 1)

items.statement: The liver contains approximately 90% of the vitamin A reserves in healthy individuals.
items.explanation: The liver stores ~90% of vitamin A reserves in healthy individuals.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 1)

items.statement: Alcohol can interfere with the conversion of retinol to retinaldehyde in the eye.
items.explanation: Alcohol competes for alcohol dehydrogenase, interfering with the conversion of retinol to retinaldehyde.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 2)

items.statement: In areas where vitamin A deficiency is prevalent, supplementation has no effect on childhood mortality.
items.explanation: Vitamin A supplementation can markedly reduce the risk of childhood mortality by 23–34%.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 2)

items.statement: Uncomplicated vitamin A deficiency is common in industrialized countries.
items.explanation: Uncomplicated vitamin A deficiency is rare in industrialized countries.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 1)

items.statement: Non-provitamin A carotenoids such as lutein and zeaxanthin have been conclusively proven to prevent macular degeneration.
items.explanation: The effectiveness of non-provitamin A carotenoids like lutein and zeaxanthin in preventing macular degeneration has not been proved by intervention studies.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 1)

items.statement: High doses of supplemental carotenoids are associated with toxic symptoms.
items.explanation: High doses of supplemental carotenoids do not result in toxic symptoms.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 2)

items.statement: Carotenemia is characterized by yellowing of the skin and sclera.
items.explanation: Carotenemia causes yellowing of the skin but not the sclera.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 2)

items.statement: Recent placebo-controlled studies have shown a therapeutic benefit of vitamin D for cancer prevention.
items.explanation: Recent placebo-controlled studies did not show a therapeutic benefit of vitamin D for cancer prevention.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIND

items.statement: The upper limit of intake for vitamin D has been set at 4000 IU/d.
items.explanation: The upper limit of intake for vitamin D is indeed set at 4000 IU/d.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIND

items.statement: Dietary deficiency of vitamin E is common in developed countries.
items.explanation: Dietary deficiency of vitamin E does not exist in developed countries.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINE

items.statement: Vitamin E acts as a chain-breaking antioxidant and protects polyunsaturated fats from oxidation.
items.explanation: Vitamin E is an efficient peroxyl radical scavenger that protects low-density lipoproteins and polyunsaturated fats in membranes from oxidation.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINE

items.statement: Vitamin E supplementation has been proven to slow the aging process.
items.explanation: Convincing evidence for vitamin E's ability to slow the aging process is lacking.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin E Deficiency | VITAMINK

items.statement: Toxicity from dietary phylloquinones and menaquinones has been described.
items.explanation: Toxicity from dietary phylloquinones and menaquinones has not been described.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin E Deficiency | VITAMINK

items.statement: Chronic large doses of zinc can lead to a secondary copper deficiency.
items.explanation: High doses of zinc may cause hypochromic anemia due to secondary copper deficiency.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · ZINC | COPPER

items.statement: Intranasal zinc preparations are safe and do not affect the nasal mucosa.
items.explanation: Intranasal zinc preparations may cause irreversible damage to the nasal mucosa and anosmia.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · ZINC | COPPER

items.subtopic: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess
items.assertion: Deficiencies of vitamins and minerals may be caused by disease states such as malabsorption.
items.reason: Therapeutic modalities can deplete essential nutrients from the body, requiring supplementation.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; however, the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess

items.assertion: Wet beriberi presents primarily with cardiovascular symptoms.
items.reason: It is due to impaired myocardial energy metabolism and dysautonomia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINS | THIAMINE (VITAMIN B $ _{1} $)

items.assertion: Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome is easily diagnosed due to its typical clinical picture.
items.reason: The syndrome includes symptoms such as horizontal nystagmus, ophthalmoplegia, cerebellar ataxia, and mental impairment.
items.explanation: Although the assertion is false (it's underdiagnosed), the reason correctly describes the symptoms.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMINS | THIAMINE (VITAMIN B $ _{1} $)

items.assertion: Pyridoxine should be given concurrently with isoniazid to avoid neuropathy.
items.reason: Isoniazid interacts with PLP due to a reaction with carbonyl groups, which can lead to neuropathy if not counteracted by pyridoxine.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B $ _{6} $)

items.assertion: Low levels of circulating vitamin B₆ have been associated with inflammation.
items.reason: Independent of homocysteine, low levels of circulating vitamin B₆ are linked to elevated levels of C-reactive protein.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · PYRIDOXINE (VITAMIN B $ _{6} $)

items.assertion: Vitamin C deficiency primarily affects connective tissue metabolism.
items.reason: Scurvy, a result of vitamin C deficiency, involves impaired formation of mature connective tissue.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FOLATE (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $) | VITAMIN C

items.assertion: High doses of vitamin C can lead to kidney stones.
items.reason: Chronic high-dose supplementation is associated with increased oxalate production, leading to stone formation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the association between high-dose vitamin C and kidney stones has not been conclusively proven in trials.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · FOLATE (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $) | VITAMIN C

items.assertion: Retinoic acid is a potent regulator of gene transcription.
items.reason: It acts through nuclear receptor signaling involving retinoic acid receptors and retinoid X receptors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why retinoic acid regulates gene transcription.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 1)

items.assertion: Retinol-binding protein is involved in vitamin A metabolism.
items.reason: It binds to transthyretin to form a complex that prevents kidney filtration of vitamin A.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the role of retinol-binding protein in preventing kidney filtration.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 1)

items.assertion: Maternal vitamin A deficiency increases the risk of maternal infection and death.
items.reason: Moderate to severe vitamin A deficiency during pregnancy is associated with increased risks for the mother.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 2)

items.assertion: High-dose vitamin A supplementation is recommended for newborns by WHO.
items.reason: Newborns in South Asia benefit from a single oral dose of vitamin A shortly after birth.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as the WHO does not recommend high-dose supplementation to newborns.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIN A (part 2)

items.assertion: Severe measles can lead to secondary vitamin A deficiency.
items.reason: Children hospitalized with measles should receive two doses of vitamin A on consecutive days.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why severe measles leads to secondary vitamin A deficiency.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 1)

items.assertion: High doses of $  eta $-carotene reduce lung cancer incidence in smokers.
items.reason: Epidemiologic studies showed diets high in $  eta $-carotene are associated with lower incidences of cancers.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as intervention studies found increased lung cancer incidence; the reason is true but does not explain the incorrect assertion.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin A Deficiency (part 1)

items.assertion: Older studies suggest that vitamin D may be useful as adjunctive treatment for multiple sclerosis.
items.reason: Vitamin D is important for maintaining normal function of many nonskeletal tissues such as muscle and immune function.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not directly explain why vitamin D may be useful for multiple sclerosis.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIND

items.assertion: High plasma levels of 1,25(OH)2 vitamin D and calcium are central features of vitamin D toxicity.
items.reason: Acute vitamin D intoxication is common due to excessive ingestion of supplements.
items.explanation: The assertion is true; however, the reason is false as acute vitamin D intoxication is rare.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · VITAMIND

items.assertion: Vitamin E deficiency in newborns can lead to hemorrhage.
items.reason: Newborns are susceptible due to low fat stores, low breast milk levels of vitamin K, and poor placental transport.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is related to vitamin K deficiency, not vitamin E.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin E Deficiency | VITAMINK

items.assertion: High doses of vitamin E can reduce platelet aggregation.
items.reason: This effect makes high-dose vitamin E contraindicated in patients taking warfarin and antiplatelet agents.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Vitamin and Trace Mineral Deficiency and Excess · Vitamin E Deficiency | VITAMINK
